request.question: Who was born earlier, Dallas Green or Brian Johnson?

title: Miniver Cheevy
Brian Johnson
Have It All (Bethel Music song)
Dallas Green (baseball)
Little Hell (album)
Richard B. Johnson
Dallas Green (musician)
Brian Johnson (pitcher)
Dallas Green Monarchs
Rose ave.
sentences: "Miniver Cheevy" is a narrative poem written by Edwin Arlington Robinson and first published in "The Town down the River" in 1910.

The poem, written in quatrains of iambic tetrameter for three lines, followed by a catalectic line of only three iambs, relates the story of a hopeless romantic who spends his days thinking about what might have been if only he had been born earlier in time.
Brian Francis Johnson (born 5 October 1947) is an English singer and songwriter.
In 1980, he became the third lead singer of the Australian rock band AC/DC, after the death of their second lead singer Bon Scott.
He and the rest of the band were inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2003.
In March 2016, Johnson stepped down from touring on the Rock or Bust World Tour due to hearing problems.

On 20 May 2016, Johnson stated in an interview "I've had a pretty good run" in AC/DC and implied that he might not be returning by saying "I'm just thankful, really, that I came out of it in one piece."
"Have It All" is a song by Bethel Music and Brian Johnson and is Bethel Music's lead and only single from their eighth live album, "Have It All" (2016).
The live version of "Have It All" was released on February 20, 2016 available on streaming, followed by the release of the studio version on May 6, 2016.
The song was written by Brian Johnson, Bobby Strand, Lindsey Strand, Mia Fieldes, and Joel Taylor, with production being handled by Chris Greely and Bobby Strand.
George Dallas Green (August 4, 1934 – March 22, 2017) was an American pitcher, manager and executive in Major League Baseball.
After playing for the Philadelphia Phillies, Washington Senators, and New York Mets from 1960 through 1967, he went on to manage the Phillies, New York Yankees, and Mets.

Green managed the Phillies when they won their first World Series title in 1980 over the Kansas City Royals, and as general manager of the Chicago Cubs from 1981 to 1987 he built the club which won a division title in 1984, the Cubs' first postseason appearance in 39 years.
Green had a losing record both as a pitcher and as a manager.
Nonetheless, in 1983 he was inducted into the Delaware Sports Museum and Hall of Fame.
He achieved notoriety for his blunt manner.
Little Hell is the third album by City and Colour, released on June 7, 2011.

Besides Dallas Green who recorded the majority of the instrumentals himself, contributing musicians on the record include Daniel Romano of Attack in Black, Dylan Green and Scott Remila of Raising the Fawn, Nick Skalkos of The Miniatures, Misha Bower of Bruce Peninsula and Anna Jarvis and Jordan Mitchell of The Rest.
The album art is an illustration of Allard Schager's photograph "Fields of Gold", taken in a tulip field outside Alkmaar in North Holland.
Richard Brian Johnson is the author of the book "Abominable Firebug" (ISBN  ), which presents his account of daily life at the Lyman School for Boys.
Johnson invented the Rubber Ducky antenna while attending the Lyman School for Boys.
Johnson went on to a career as an engineer and inventor.
He also created the JMODEM file transfer protocol.
Mr. Johnson founded the Danvers, Massachusetts, software company, Route 495 Software, LLC, in early 2009.
Johnson is also an activist for civil rights issues, and has communicated with the President of the United States on issues involving general aviation.
Johnson has continued to give talks to groups interested in learning about the nation's first reform school.
Dallas Michael John Albert Green (born September 29, 1980) is a Canadian musician and singer-songwriter who records under the alias City and Colour.
He plays melodic acoustic and folk music and is often accompanied by a rotating number of Canadian indie rock musicians, such as Spencer Burton.
He is also known for his contributions as the lead singer, rhythm guitarist, and songwriter for the post-hardcore band Alexisonfire.
In 2005, he debuted his first full-length album, "Sometimes", which achieved platinum certification in 2006.
City and Colour began performing in small intimate venues between Alexisonfire tours.
The name "City and Colour" comes from his own name: "Dallas", a city, and "Green", a colour.
His reasoning for the name was that he felt queasy "putting the album out under the name Dallas Green".
Christopher Brian Johnson (born December 7, 1990) is an American professional baseball pitcher for the Boston Red Sox of Major League Baseball (MLB).

Johnson was part of the No. 1 recruiting class by Baseball America at the University of Florida and was named a freshman All-American by "Baseball America", Yahoo! Sports, National Collegiate Baseball Writers Association, and Louisville Slugger in 2010.
Johnson also played for the collegiate national Team USA baseball squad in the summer of 2010.
Johnson was one of only two freshmen in the entire country, the other being Gator teammate Nolan Fontana, to play for Team USA.
The Dallas Green Monarchs were a semipro baseball team in the Negro Leagues from 1940-1947 and again in 1953.
The team was ostensibly the successor of the Dallas Black Giants which had disbanded after the 1938 season.
The 1940s team played the majority of their games at Rebel Field while the 1953 team played at Burnett Field.
From 1940-1942, their intracity "rival" was the Dallas Wonders who they played for the city tournament in 1940.
The Green Monarchs won the Dixie Negro Semipro tournament in 1947 and advanced to the National Semipro Congress.
Chicago Cubs' great Ernie Banks' was a bat boy for the Green Monarchs and his father Eddie Banks was its catcher.
Green Monarch Hank "Donkey" Thompson went on to Major League Baseball playing for the New York Giants.
The 1953 team played in the North Texas Negro Baseball League.
But the 1954 and 1955 Dallas team in that League was the Dallas Bluebirds and the Green Monarchs appear to have been dissolved.
rose ave. (also known as Rose Ave. or roseave.)
is the debut studio album by folk music duo You+Me, consisting of Dallas Green and Alecia Moore (Pink).
The album was released on October 14, 2014 through RCA and Dine Alone records.
All songs were written and produced by Pink and Green except "No Ordinary Love", a song by English band Sade.
The album debuted at number-one on the Canadian Albums Chart and at number-four on the "Billboard" 200.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Who was born earlier, Dallas Green or Brian Johnson?
hop1_docs: Brian Johnson (footballer, born 1948) | Brian Joseph Johnson (born 29 October 1948) is an English footballer, who played as a midfielder in the Football League for Tranmere Rovers.

Brian Price (American football, born 1994) | Brian Keith Price (born June 24, 1994) is an American football defensive tackle for the Dallas Cowboys of the National Football League (NFL).  He played college football at Texas-San Antonio, and was signed by the Green Bay Packers as an undrafted free agent in 2016.

Brian Johnson (footballer, born 1955) | Brian Johnson (born 21 October 1955) is an English retired footballer who played as a winger.  He made 197 appearances in the Football League for Plymouth Argyle, scoring 40 goals, and a further seven while on loan at Torquay United.

Brian Johnson (ice hockey) | Brian Johnson (born April 1, 1960 in Montreal, Quebec) is a retired Canadian professional ice hockey right winger who played one season in the National Hockey League for the Detroit Red Wings.

Brian Johnson (fullback) | Brian "Horse" Johnson (born April 6, 1979) is a former American football fullback who played five seasons with the San Jose SaberCats of the Arena Football League (AFL).  He played college football at the University of New Mexico.
Brian Rogers (baseball) | Brian Alan Rogers (born July 17, 1982 in Dallas, Texas) is a former relief pitcher in Major League Baseball who played for the Pittsburgh Pirates in 2006 and 2007.
Brian Johnson (athlete) | Brian Johnson (born March 5, 1980 in Iowa, Louisiana) is an American long jumper.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Brian Johnson (footballer, born 1948) — [1] "born 29 October 1948"
- Brian Johnson (footballer, born 1955) — [3] "born 21 October 1955"
- Brian Johnson (ice hockey) — [4] "born April 1, 1960"
- Brian "Horse" Johnson (fullback) — [5] "born April 6, 1979"
- Brian Johnson (athlete) — [7] "born March 5, 1980"

Ambiguities:
- "Brian Johnson" corresponds to multiple candidates: "Brian Johnson (footballer, born 1948)","Brian Johnson (footballer, born 1955)","Brian Johnson (ice hockey)","Brian \"Horse\" Johnson (fullback)","Brian Johnson (athlete)"
- "Dallas Green" is not present in the provided passages (no candidate labels or dates).

Bridge / missing fact:
- Missing Dallas Green birth date (needed to compare birth years).
- Missing which specific "Brian Johnson" the question intends (must select one of the candidates above to compare).

Retrieval direction:
- Fetch page "Dallas Green (musician)" or run query "Dallas Green birth date" to get his birth year.
- Fetch page "Dallas Green (baseball)" if the user might mean the baseball manager (query "Dallas Green birth date baseball").
- Fetch the disambiguation page "Brian Johnson" or "Brian Johnson (disambiguation)" to confirm which Brian Johnson is intended.
- If likely comparing to AC/DC singer, fetch "Brian Johnson (singer)" (query "Brian Johnson birth date AC/DC").
Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets above.
hop2_docs: Brian Price (American football, born 1994) | Brian Keith Price (born June 24, 1994) is an American football defensive tackle for the Dallas Cowboys of the National Football League (NFL).  He played college football at Texas-San Antonio, and was signed by the Green Bay Packers as an undrafted free agent in 2016.
Brian Johnson (athlete) | Brian Johnson (born March 5, 1980 in Iowa, Louisiana) is an American long jumper.
Brian Johnson (footballer, born 1948) | Brian Joseph Johnson (born 29 October 1948) is an English footballer, who played as a midfielder in the Football League for Tranmere Rovers.
Brian Johnson (disambiguation) | Brian Johnson (born 1947) is British lead singer of Australian hard rock band AC/DC.
Brian Johnson (special effects artist) | Brian Johnson (born 1939 or 1940) is a British designer and director of film and television special effects.

2004 Dallas Burn season | The 2004 Dallas Burn season was the eighth season of the Major League Soccer team.  The season saw the team fail to make the playoffs for the second consecutive year.  The season was also the first full season under head coach Colin Clarke.  The team moved from Dragon Stadium back to the Cotton Bowl.  It would be the last season for the team in the Cotton Bowl, as they would move to their current stadium in Frisco.  It was also the team’s final season as the Burn.  With the move to their own stadium the next year, the team would be rebranded as FC Dallas in 2005.  Burn forward Eddie Johnson shared the Golden Boot Award with Brian Ching, tying him for the most goals in the MLS with 12.  Johnson was the second Burn player to win the award, with Jason Kreis previously winning it in 1999.
Brian Johnson (catcher) | Brian David Johnson (born January 8, 1968 in Oakland, California) is a retired Major League Baseball catcher and former quarterback for Stanford University.
response.summary_2: The provided passages list birth years for multiple Brian Johnsons (including Brian Johnson (born 1947) and Brian Johnson (footballer, born 29 October 1948)) but give no birth date for Dallas Green, so the comparison is unclear from these sources.
